en.extract:
The red-capped manakin (Ceratopipra mentalis) is a species of bird in the family Pipridae.
It is found in Belize, Colombia, Costa Rica, Ecuador, Guatemala, Honduras, Mexico, Nicaragua, Peru and Panama.
Its natural habitat is subtropical or tropical moist lowland forest.
The bird is probably best known for the male's unusual courting method whereby he shuffles rapidly backwards across a branch, akin to a speedy moonwalk. Hence its nickname the Michael Jackson bird.


Taxonomy
The red-capped manakin was formally described and illustrated in 1857 by the English zoologist Philip Sclater based on specimen collected by Auguste Sallé in Córdoba, Veracruz, southern Mexico. Sclater placed the species in the genus Pipra and coined the binomial name Pipra mentalis. The specific epithet mentalis is Latin meaning "pertaining to the chin".
The red-capped manakin was moved to the genus Ceratopipra when molecular phylogenetic studies found that Pipra was non-monophyletic.  It is closely related to, and in eastern Panama sometimes hybridizes with, the golden-headed manakin.
Three subspecies are recognized subspecies:

Ceratopipra mentalis mentalis (Sclater, PL, 1857) – southeast Mexico to east Costa Rica
Ceratopipra mentalis ignifera (Bangs, 1901) – west Costa Rica and west Panama
Ceratopipra mentalis minor (Hartert, 1898) – east Panama, west Colombia and northwest Ecuador


Description

The red-capped manakin is a small passerine, measuring 4 in (10 cm) in length and weighing 16 g (0.56 oz). The male is velvety black apart from a bright red head and nape, bright yellow thighs, and a pale yellow chin and wing linings. The female is olive green above, with paler, more yellow-green underparts. Both sexes have dull brown legs. The male's irides are white, while those of the female and young are brown.
While the adult male is distinctive, the female and youngsters can be confused with several similar species. The male golden-collared manakin is larger, and has orange (rather than brown) legs, while the female velvety manakin is a brighter green (rather than olive).


Habitat and range
Found primarily in humid forest and second growth woodland, the red-capped manakin typically occurs below 400–500 m (1,300–1,600 ft) above sea level, though it sometimes ranges as high as 900 m (3,000 ft).  Most are resident, but some individuals are known to migrate to take advantage of changing food resources: the number of red-capped manakins caught in mist nets at La Selva Biological Reserve, in eastern Costa Rica, tripled in January and February, when a favored fruit ripened, for example, while the number caught at a nearby higher elevation site (where the fruits were not found) dropped to zero.


Behavior


Food and feeding
The red-capped manakin is a frugivore, feeding almost exclusively on fruits.  These pass very quickly through the bird's digestive system, typically taking less than 18 minutes to process. The manakins ability to process large quantities of fruit have evolved to be fast due to experiencing certain seasons where there is not an abundant amount of fruits. Therefore, they had to consume large volumes of food to get the nutrients they need. Seeds from a variety of plants are consumed; one study in Costa Rica found evidence of 70 species, including those from the genera Clidemia, Hampea, Henriettea, Leandra, Miconia, Ossaea, Pinzona and Psychotria, in the fecal droppings of red-capped manakins.


Notes


References


Further reading


External links

Animated .gif of the "moonwalk"

The '''red-capped manakin''' ('''''Ceratopipra mentalis''''') is a species of [[bird]] in the family [[Pipridae]].
It is found in [[Belize]], [[Colombia]], [[Costa Rica]], [[Ecuador]], [[Guatemala]], [[Honduras]], [[Mexico]], [[Nicaragua]], [[Peru]] and [[Panama]].
Its natural [[habitat]] is [[subtropical or tropical moist lowland forest]].

The bird is probably best known for the male's unusual courting method whereby he shuffles rapidly backwards across a branch, akin to a speedy [[Moonwalk (dance)|moonwalk]]. Hence its nickname the Michael Jackson bird.

==Taxonomy==
The red-capped manakin was [[species description|formally described]] and illustrated in 1857 by the English zoologist [[Philip Sclater]] based on specimen collected by [[Auguste Sallé]] in [[Córdoba, Veracruz]], southern Mexico. Sclater placed the species in the genus ''[[Pipra]]'' and coined the [[binomial name]] ''Pipra mentalis''.<ref>{{ cite journal | last=Sclater | first=Philip Lutley| author-link=Philip Sclater | year=1856 | title=Calatlogue of the birds collected by M. Auguste Sallé in southern Mexico, with descriptions of new species | journal=Proceedings of the Zoological Society of London | volume=Part 24 | issue=319 | pages=283–311 [299, Plate 121] | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/12860851 }} Although the volume is dated 1856 on the title page, the article was not published until 1857.</ref><ref>{{ cite book | editor-last=Traylor | editor-first=Melvin A. Jr | author-link=Melvin Alvah Traylor Jr. | year=1979 | title=Check-List of Birds of the World | volume=8 | publisher=Museum of Comparative Zoology  | place=Cambridge, Massachusetts  | page=278 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/14501055 }}</ref> The specific epithet ''mentalis'' is [[Latin]] meaning "pertaining to the chin".<ref>{{cite book | last=Jobling | first=James A. | year=2010| title=The Helm Dictionary of Scientific Bird Names | publisher=Christopher Helm | location=London | isbn=978-1-4081-2501-4 | page=250 }}</ref>

The red-capped manakin was moved to the genus ''[[Ceratopipra]]'' when [[molecular phylogenetic]] studies found that ''Pipra'' was [[monophyletic|non-monophyletic]].<ref>{{ cite journal | last1=Ohlson | first1=J.I. | last2=Fjeldså | first2=J. | last3=Ericson | first3=P.G.P. | year=2013 | title=Molecular phylogeny of the manakins (Aves: Passeriformes: Pipridae), with a new classification and the description of a new genus | journal=Molecular Phylogenetics and Evolution | volume=69 | issue=3 | pages=796–804 | doi=10.1016/j.ympev.2013.06.024 | pmid=23831559 }}</ref><ref name=ioc>{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela Rasmussen | date=July 2020 | title=Cotingas, manakins, tityras, becards | work=IOC World Bird List Version 10.2 | url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/cotingas/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=15 December 2020 }}</ref>  It is closely related to, and in eastern Panama sometimes hybridizes with, the [[golden-headed manakin]].<ref name=Hilty/>

Three [[subspecies]] are recognized subspecies:<ref name=ioc/>
* ''Ceratopipra mentalis mentalis'' ([[Philip Sclater|Sclater]], PL, 1857) – southeast Mexico to east Costa Rica
* ''Ceratopipra mentalis ignifera'' ([[Outram Bangs|Bangs]], 1901) – west Costa Rica and west Panama
* ''Ceratopipra mentalis minor'' ([[Ernst Hartert|Hartert]], 1898) – east Panama, west Colombia and northwest Ecuador

==Description==
[[File:PipraMentalisWolf.jpg|thumb]]
The red-capped manakin is a small passerine, measuring {{convert|4|in|cm|abbr=on}} in length<ref name=Skutch/>{{#tag:ref|By convention, length is measured from the tip of the bill to the tip of the tail on a dead bird (or skin) laid on its back.<ref>{{cite book |title=Handbook of the Birds of Europe, the Middle East and North Africa: [[Birds of the Western Palearctic]], Volume 1, Ostrich to Ducks |publisher=Oxford University Press |year=1977 |isbn=0-19-857358-8 |editor=Cramp, Stanley |page=3}}</ref>|group="nb"}} and weighing {{convert|16|g|oz|abbr=on}}. The male is velvety black apart from a bright red head and nape, bright yellow thighs, and a pale yellow chin and wing linings.<ref name=Skutch>{{cite book |title=A Guide to the Birds of Costa Rica |first1=F. Gary |last1=Stiles |first2=Alexander F |last2=Skutch |year=1989 |publisher=Cornell University Press |pages=299–300 |isbn=0-8014-9600-4}}</ref> The female is olive green above, with paler, more yellow-green underparts. Both sexes have dull brown legs. The male's [[iris (eye)|irides]] are white, while those of the female and young are brown.<ref name=Hilty>{{cite book |title=A Guide to the Birds of Colombia |first=Steven |last=Hilty |year=1986 |location=Princeton, NJ |publisher=Princeton University Press |isbn=0-691-08372-X |page=430 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=kHa6tJNKGDAC&pg=PA430}}</ref>

While the adult male is distinctive, the female and youngsters can be confused with several similar species. The male [[golden-collared manakin]] is larger, and has orange (rather than brown) legs, while the female [[velvety manakin]] is a brighter green (rather than olive).<ref name=Hilty/>

==Habitat and range==
Found primarily in humid forest and second growth woodland, the red-capped manakin typically occurs below {{convert|400|–|500|m|ft|abbr=on}} above sea level, though it sometimes ranges as high as {{convert|900|m|ft|abbr=on}}.<ref name=Hilty/>  Most are resident, but some individuals are known to migrate to take advantage of changing food resources: the number of red-capped manakins caught in [[mist net]]s at [[La Selva Biological Reserve]], in eastern [[Costa Rica]], tripled in January and February, when a favored fruit ripened, for example, while the number caught at a nearby higher elevation site (where the fruits were not found) dropped to zero.<ref>{{cite book |title=Birds of Tropical America: A Watcher's Introduction to Behavior, Breeding, and Diversity |first1=Steven L. |last1=Hilty |first2=Mimi Hoppe |last2=Wolf |pages=89–90 |isbn=0-292-70673-1 |year=2005 |publisher=University of Texas Press}}</ref>

==Behavior==

===Food and feeding===
The red-capped manakin is a [[frugivore]], feeding almost exclusively on fruits.  These pass very quickly through the bird's digestive system, typically taking less than 18 minutes to process.<ref>{{cite journal |journal=The Condor |title=Nocturnal Hypothermia and Oxygen Consumption in Manakins |first1=Theresa L. |last1=Bucher |first2=Andrea |last2=Worthington |pages=327–331 |volume=84 |issue=3 |date=May–June 1982 |url=http://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/condor/v084n03/p0327-p0331.pdf |doi=10.2307/1367377|jstor=1367377 }}</ref> The manakins ability to process large quantities of fruit have evolved to be fast due to experiencing certain seasons where there is not an abundant amount of fruits. Therefore, they had to consume large volumes of food to get the nutrients they need.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Worthington |first1=Andrea |title=Adaptations for Avian Frugivory: Assimilation Efficiency and Gut Transit Time of Manacus vitellinus and Pipra mentalis |journal=Oecologia |date=1989 |volume=80 |issue=3 |page=381-389 |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/4219061 |access-date=4 November 2024}}</ref> Seeds from a variety of plants are consumed; one study in Costa Rica found evidence of 70 species, including those from the genera ''[[Clidemia]]'', ''[[Hampea]]'', ''[[Henriettea]]'', ''[[Leandra]]'', ''[[Miconia]]'', ''[[Ossaea]]'', ''[[Pinzona]]'' and ''[[Psychotria]]'', in the fecal droppings of red-capped manakins.<ref>{{cite book |title=Seed dispersal: theory and its application in a changing world |editor1-first=Andrew J |editor1-last=Dennis |editor2-first=Eugene W |editor2-last=Schupp |editor3-first=Ronda J. |editor3-last=Green |editor4-first=David A |editor4-last=Westcott |page=184 |isbn=978-1-84593-165-0 |publisher=CABI |location=Wallingford, UK |year=2007 |contribution=Ecological Redundancy in Seed Dispersal Systems: A Comparison Between Manakins (Aves Pipridae) in Two Tropical Forests |first1=Bette A. |last1=Loiselle |first2=Pedro G. |last2=Blendinger |first3=John G. |last3=Blake |first4=Thomas B. |last4=Ryder |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ilTrraJlLsMC&pg=PA184}}</ref>
